data.title: Nike's stock dives, as sales warning shows turnaround plan still isn't working
data.description:
By Bill Peters

'This is complex work, and parts of it are taking longer than I'd like,' CEO says of Nike's rebound efforts

Nike reported quarterly results on Tuesday afternoon.

Shares of Nike sank 9% after hours on Tuesday, as executives at the sneaker giant forecast more falling sales in the months ahead and conceded that the company's turnaround efforts were taking longer than they'd wanted.

Management made the forecast as gains in running shoes, soccer gear and a rebound in North America run up against an array of other problems. Those issues include efforts to sell off an excess of casual sneakers that have fallen out of style; weakness in sportswear; U.S. tariffs that continue to fluctuate; struggles in China, and plans to address them that will likely hurt sales.

"This is complex work, and parts of it are taking longer than I'd like," CEO Elliott Hill said of Nike's comeback efforts on the company's earnings call.

In North America, management said it was not yet seeing any signs of consumer hesitation as the Iran war drives up gas prices and rattles markets. But it did say disruptions in the Middle East and heavy discounting in Europe could leave it with extra unsold product in those regions.

Nike (NKE) is trying to recover from weaker demand in recent years, as higher prices for basics steer people away from sneaker purchases, and as it squares off against rival brands like Hoka and On Running. The company's stock as of last week hovered at nine-year lows. On Monday, the stock closed at its lowest price since October 2017.

Analysts have debated the state of Nike's efforts to cut costs, make more products that suit athletes' needs, and push further into running shoes and women's apparel. But ahead of Tuesday's results, UBS analysts said that data showed some of Nike's newer offerings weren't catching on with customers.

Hill on Tuesday said that Nike expects to finish taking actions related to its turnaround by the end of the calendar year. But through the end of that period, Nike also said it expects revenue to be down in the low-single-digit percentage range.

For its fiscal fourth quarter, which runs through May, Nike expects sales to fall 2% to 4%, weighed down by weaker trends in its Converse segment and difficulties in China, whose economy has been shaky.

In December, Nike said it had underinvested in stores in China, and had thrown itself too heavily into the discounting fray there, rather than trying to position itself as a hub for premium products. Nike is currently testing out new stores in the region and is trying to clear out older products, and it has brought in new leadership for its business there. But as it tries to make improvements in China through the next fiscal year - its fiscal 2027 - management said sales growth there would likely be more subdued as a result.

Nike on Tuesday reported quarterly results that were a bit better than Wall Street's expectations. But Hill said progress was "different" across various areas of the business.

Nike said that for its fiscal third quarter that ran through February, sales were flat from the previous year at $11.28 billion, as gains in North America offset continued struggles in China. The company earned 35 cents a share, down from 54 cents a share the prior year.

The average estimates among analysts polled by FactSet were for sales of $11.23 billion and earnings per share of 29 cents.

Sales for Nike's business with outside retailers rose 5% during the quarter. However, sales at its own physical and online stores fell 4%. Gross margin, a measure of the profitability of sales, fell to 40.2%, from 41.5%.

After trying to sell more products itself over recent years, Nike has tried to work more with retail chains to recharge sales growth. And after competition and discounting hurt online sales, the company has tried to turn its own stores and its e-commerce business into a destination for higher-end, full-priced products.

Under Hill's leadership, Nike has also tried to thin down or sell off its stockpiles of its classic casual sneakers, like Dunks and Air Force 1s, whose popularity has waned after Nike flooded the market with them. But those efforts weighed on results. Hill, on Tuesday, said that by the end of Nike's current fiscal year, "we will have intentionally reduced over $4 billion of revenue from the peak levels of classic footwear franchises."

Nike's results also came after the Supreme Court in February struck down President Donald Trump's emergency-powers tariffs. Last year, Nike said it faced an extra $1.5 billion in tariff-related costs.

On Tuesday, Nike Chief Financial Officer Matthew Friend said barring any major changes to the U.S.-led trade war, the first quarter of the company's next fiscal year would likely be the last quarter in which higher tariffs would be a significant threat to gross margins.

He also said that Nike would resume offering full-year and long-term financial forecasts at its investor day in the fall.

As of Tuesday's close, Nike's stock had dropped 16.8% over the past 12 months, while the S&P 500 index SPX had gained 16.3%.

-Bill Peters

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Bayer Weighs U.S. Pharma Expansion as Turnaround Picks Up Speed — Interview
data.description:
(11:11 GMT) *Bayer Pharma President Says Business Is Looking at U.S. Manufacturing Expansion
(11:11 GMT) *Bayer Pharma President Says Business Has Shifted to U.S.-Centric Approach
(11:11 GMT) *Bayer Is Looking at Pharma Acquisitions, Unit President Says
(11:11 GMT) *Bayer Is in Negotiations for a Number of Pharma Deals, Unit President Says
(11:11 GMT) *Bayer Pharma President Rules Out Asset Sales Until Recent Launches Make Progress
(11:11 GMT) Bayer Weighs U.S. Pharma Expansion as Turnaround Picks Up Speed — Interview

By Adria Calatayud

Bayer is looking at expanding its pharmaceutical manufacturing footprint in the U.S., the head of its pharma unit said, as the business turns to the world's largest drug market to fuel a turnaround.

The pharma division of the German conglomerate is trying to turn a corner after the patent expiration of its two top-selling drugs hurt sales and earnings over the past couple of years, while uncertainty about legal battles and debt-reduction efforts at the broader group limited its financial headroom.

Stefan Oelrich, the president of Bayer's pharma division, said the business is leaving the patent cliff behind and poised to grow again.

The company has guided for pharma sales to rise by mid single percentage digits starting next year into the end of the decade and profitability toward 30% by 2030. Last year, the unit posted sales growth of 2% excluding currency movements and an adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization margin of 25.4%.

"In our turnaround story, the U.S. has played a central role," Oelrich said in an interview.

When Bayer's pharma business was facing the biggest patent cliff in its history, the company looked to the U.S. for a boost. It kept the rights in the country for the medicines it saw as its next growth drivers and bought a trio of U.S. biotech companies to fill its pipeline. The company shared rights of its two blockbuster drugs that recently went off patent, blood thinner Xarelto and eye drug Eylea, with Johnson & Johnson and Regeneron Pharmaceuticals, respectively.

"We built a commercial footprint that we didn't have in cardiovascular, we already were present to some degree in oncology, which we've broadened significantly since, and the success proves that this is the right strategy," Oelrich said. "Today our growth comes predominantly from the U.S."

Bayer is now counting on five drugs that have potential to become blockbusters or have already achieved that status--Nubeqa for prostate cancer, Kerendia for chronic kidney disease and heart failure, Beyonttra for a heart disease, Lynkuet for menopause, and asundexian for stroke prevention. Oelrich said U.S. approval for asundexian is expected either toward the end of this year or the beginning of next, followed by authorization in Europe next year.

The company has adopted a U.S.-centric approach to its pharma portfolio on sales and marketing, and its research-and-development investment is now more balanced between the U.S. and Europe as well, Oelrich said. Manufacturing could be next.

"With the geopolitical shifts that we're seeing, we're also looking at expanding potentially the footprint," he said.

Several big pharma companies last year set out plans to invest dozens of billions of dollars in the U.S. over the coming years, as the threat of tariffs and moves by the Trump administration to lower medicine prices loomed over the industry.

Oelrich said the company is also considering acquisitions and that negotiations are under way for a number of deals.

"We're looking at this, but we're picky," he said. "We have a lot of [medicine] modalities for a company of our size, so I don't necessarily need to add on that side. I'm more looking now at adding molecules rather than companies, or maybe companies that only hold one or two molecules."

Asset sales are off the table for now, but the business might reconsider strategic options once its recently launched products are more mature, Oelrich said.

Shortly after Oelrich became Bayer's pharma head, the company sold its animal-health business. It also acquired BlueRock Therapeutics, AskBio and Vividion Therapeutics, adding cell and gene therapy technologies as well as drug candidates in oncology and other therapeutic areas like Parkinson's disease.

These are risky bets, but offer tremendous potential if they break through, Oelrich said. The company is focusing on cancer, cardiovascular and neurodegenerative diseases, areas where it sees the biggest unmet needs in the world, he added.

"I think those are the right fields to be in and this is where the next big thing hopefully is going to wait for us somewhere in the future," Oelrich said.

Write to Adria Calatayud at adria.calatayud@wsj.com

data.title: goeasy Ltd reports results for the quarter ended December 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• goeasy Ltd reported a quarterly adjusted loss of C$8.93​​ per share for the quarter ended December 31, lower than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of C$4.45. The mean expectation of ten analysts for the quarter was for a loss of C$3.92 per share. Wall Street expected results to range from C$-9.91 to C$4.71 per share.

  • Revenue rose 0.5% to C$406.31 million from a year ago; analysts expected C$419.12 million.

  • goeasy Ltd's reported EPS for the quarter was a loss of C$20.49​.

  • The company reported a quarterly loss of C$336.94 million.

  • goeasy Ltd shares had held steady so far this quarter and lost 70.9% so far this year.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had fallen by about 183.8% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, seven analysts negatively revised earnings estimates

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "hold" and the breakdown of recommendations is 4 "strong buy" or "buy," 5 "hold" and 1 "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the consumer lending peer group is "buy."

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for goeasy Ltd is C$62.50, about 38.8% above its last closing price of C$38.23

This summary was machine generated from LSEG data April 1 at 11:11 a.m. UTC. ​All figures in Canadian dollars unless otherwise stated. data.title: Farmers Shift to Soybeans; EV Rust Belt
data.description:
Sponsored by

Farmers Shift to Soybeans as War Boosts Fertilizer Prices; America Now Has an EV Rust Belt By Mark R. Long | WSJ Logistics Report

More U.S. farmers are shifting to planting soybeans this year, a less fertilizer-intense crop than corn, as fertilizer costs surge because of the war in Iran . American farmers intend to plant 84.7 million soybean acres in 2026, up 4% from last year, according to the U.S. Department of Agriculture.

The Wall Street Journal's Patrick Thomas writes that farmers are expected to plant around 95.3 million acres of corn, down 3% from last year. Agriculture industry officials had generally expected more soybean acres to be planted as farmers rotated from a massive corn crop in 2025. A trade deal with China and new rules mandating more soybean oil be blended into diesel

also helped lift soybean prices. Meanwhile, farmers are set to plant the smallest wheat crop

since the Agriculture Department began keeping track in 1919.

It has been nearly a month since a vessel loaded with fertilizer left the Persian Gulf , which has become increasingly crowded with them, the Journal's James T. Areddy reports. As of Tuesday, 36 ships were loading or laden with about 1.65 million tons of fertilizers in the Gulf, according to Kpler, up from the 23 vessels the shipping intelligence firm estimated in a March 11 report.

Brent crude oil futures surged 63% in March, the global benchmark's largest one-month percentage gain

on record. (WSJ) President Trump told aides he is willing to end the U.S. military campaign

against Iran even if the Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed. (WSJ) The aircraft carrier USS George H.W. Bush and its accompanying warships are deploying to the Middle East , joining the USS Abraham Lincoln and the USS Gerald R. Ford carrier strike groups. (WSJ) Malaysia said Iran assured it that its vessels will receive safe, toll-free passage

through the Strait of Hormuz. (WSJ) Direct air-freighter and widebody capacity between Europe and Asia rose 31% in the week ended March 21

from the week ended Feb. 28, the day the Iran war began. (Air Cargo News) CONTENT FROM: PENSKE Gain an Edge. Gain Ground With Penske.

Penske's industry-first Catalyst AI(TM) uses advanced algorithms and billions of data points to compare your fleet to similar fleets. This provides your business with unique benchmarks across key metrics, like fuel efficiency, fleet utilization and maintenance-giving you a personalized game plan for improvement.

Learn More

Number of the Day Transportation Technology

Voltify raised $30 million in seed funding to trial its system that retrofits diesel locomotives with battery power

and charges them on the go via a network of microgrids.

The company says it has signed a paid pilot agreement with one of the Class 1 railroads, the Journal's Rhiannon Hoyle reports. Voltify will use the money to gear up for a real-world test of its technology just as conflict in the Middle East is sending diesel prices soaring.

The Philadelphia-based startup estimates it can cut energy costs by more than a fifth compared with the lowest price rail companies paid for diesel in the past decade.

Automotive Supply Chains

Magna International's contract to make battery enclosures for GM's new electric pickup was so big and promising that North America's largest auto-parts supplier built a new factory in Michigan. Five years on, that million-square-foot plant sits mostly empty, one of dozens of U.S. EV parts plants left desolate

by America's breakup with electric vehicles.

The WSJ's Sharon Terlep writes that Magna and its big Detroit customers are forging ahead with plans to roll back EV investments, even as the war in Iran has pushed gas prices up enough to raise enthusiasts' hopes of a comeback. GM said this week it would idle the Detroit factory where it builds the big electric trucks that Magna supplies, due to weak demand.

The unpredictability of any EV revival is reverberating through the auto industry's sprawling supply chain. Multinational companies such as Magna, Dana and BorgWarner slashed jobs and closed plants because of the EV pullback, while a string of smaller manufacturers shut down altogether. Last year, more than $20 billion in previously announced investments in EV and battery facilities were wiped out, according to Atlas Public Policy.

Zhejiang Geely Holding Group's Volvo Car will increase its stake in EV maker Polestar to 19.9% and consolidate Polestar 3 production

at its South Carolina plant. (WSJ) DoorDash invested in EV company

Also as part of a $200 million Series C financing round led by Greenoaks. (WSJ) Quotable In Other News U.S. job openings slipped in February, and hiring fell to its lowest level since April 2020 , the Labor Department said. (WSJ) The Conference Board's economic sentiment index rose to 91.8 in March , up from 91 in February, defying analyst expectations of a decline as the Iran war continues. (WSJ) U.S. home-price growth slowed in January, with the S&P Cotality Case-Shiller National Home Price Index rising 0.9% annually , compared with a 1.1% increase in December. (WSJ) Canada's GDP rose 0.1% in January and an estimated 0.2% in February, marking three months of expansion . (WSJ) Japan's economy is flashing mixed signals , with inflation cooling slightly in Tokyo and industrial output falling. (WSJ) Eurozone consumer prices rose 2.5%

in March, the fastest pace since January 2025, driven by energy prices due to the Iran war. (WSJ) Unilever agreed to combine its food business with McCormick in a deal creating a new sauces-to-spices behemoth

valued at more than $65 billion, including debt. (WSJ) Virtus Minerals finalized its acquisition of Chemaf, one of the world's biggest cobalt producers

not already in China's hands, securing supplies of the critical metal in the Democratic Republic of Congo. (WSJ) Rheinmetall and Boeing's Australian business struck a deal

to offer autonomous combat aircraft for the German armed forces. (WSJ) A three-judge appeals-court panel rejected the World Shipping Council's challenge to a U.S. law that prevents carriers from unfairly denying space

on ships to exporters. (Journal of Commerce) The federal Endangered Species Committee, in its first meeting in over 30 years, exempted Gulf of Mexico oil and gas drilling

from endangered species protections, citing national security. (Bloomberg) Amazon is adding over 1,500 FedEx Office locations to its returns network , allowing shoppers to make returns without a box or printed label. (Axios) Hanwha Ocean named Exxon Mobil veteran James Sagar as CEO of its shipping unit , and as president of a new division working on maritime technology and decarbonization. (TradeWinds) Ryder System is participating in initial fleet trials of International Motors' second-generation autonomous LT Series tractors. (Transport Topics) About Us

Mark R. Long is editor of WSJ Logistics Report. Reach him at [mark.long@wsj.com]. Follow the WSJ Logistics Report team on LinkedIn: Mark R. Long , Liz Young and Paul Berger .

This article is a text version of a Wall Street Journal newsletter published earlier today.

data.title: Beyond Meat Shares Fall as Fresh Accounting Issues Force Delay of Annual Report
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Beyond Meat shares tumbled Wednesday in premarket trading after the company said that it won't file its annual report by the prescribed deadline after discovering more accounting problems.

Shares fell 9% ahead of the morning bell at 64 cents.

The plant-based protein company late Tuesday said it found an additional material weakness in its internal controls over financial reporting, this time regarding the company's accounting for inventory provisions, and said it therefore won't be able to file its annual report.

The company said it found mistakes in its previously released 2025 quarterly results, having reported its cost of goods sold and its selling and administrative expenses as lower than they actually were in each of the first three quarters of 2025. It also reported a larger impairment loss than it should have in the third quarter, it said.

Beyond Meat delayed its earnings report at least twice in March after it said it had found errors in previously issued financial statements, and now it says it needs more time to complete its fourth-quarter and year-end financial-close procedures.

The company didn't disclose when it expects to file the form.

In the fourth quarter, revenue fell 20% to $61.6 million, missing analyst forecasts due to weak category demand and lower sales of chicken and burger products to restaurant customers, which it said weighed on the total volume of products sold.

Beyond Meat posted a net profit of $409.9 million, and a loss of 29 cents on a per share basis, in the quarter ended Dec. 31, compared with a loss of $44.9 million, or 65 cents a share, a year earlier.

The company logged a $548.7 million noncash gain due to a debt restructuring, which bolstered income in the quarter.

Looking ahead to the first quarter, the company expects revenue to be between $57 million and $59 million.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com

data.title: Poland Manufacturing Downturn Eases In March
data.description:
WARSAW (dpa-AFX) - Poland's factory activity logged a softer contraction in March amid a renewed growth in output, survey data from S&P Global showed on Wednesday.

The manufacturing purchasing managers' index, or PMI, rose to 48.7 in March from 47.1 in February. However, any PMI reading below 50 suggests contraction in the sector.

Among components, slower declines were seen in new orders and input stocks and longer suppliers' delivery times. On the other hand, output volumes increased for the first time in eleven months, though the rate of growth was weak due to lower incoming work, linked to unfavorable market conditions and uncertainty regarding the war in the Middle East.

As a result, manufacturers reduced their workforce numbers further, and the rate of job shedding was the sharpest since September 2023.

On the price front, input price inflation accelerated to the highest since October 2022 as the impact of the war in the Middle East led to higher costs for energy, fuel, and commodities. Selling price inflation also rose to the strongest since February 2023.

Looking ahead, firms remained positive about output expectations over the next twelve months, supported by increased demand, market recovery, and company developments, including investment in new production capacity and the acquisition of new customers.

Copyright(c) 2026 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved
